coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and properties of carbon quantum dots: Antioxidant, antibacterial and pH response monitoring applications
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Peng, Jingyi
Hu, Chengjun
Zhou, Xiaohan
Wu, Dingyu
Sun, Xun
Tang, Anqi
Long, Xing-Yu
Li, Xiaoqiang
Peng, Jie
Tian, Junlong
coredata.dc:description: The pH value of a wound during the healing process significantly impacts the speed and quality of healing. Real-time monitoring of the wound's healing status is crucial for doctors to assess infection and healing conditions and provide appropriate treatment plans. The carbon nanoparticles prepared in this study exhibit good fluorescence properties and pH-responsive characteristics, showing a good linear correlation in the pH range of 5.0 to 9.5. They also possess strong antioxidant and antibacterial properties, making them a promising nanomaterial for real-time pH monitoring and the promotion of wound healing. By increasing the contents of sulfur (S) and nitrogen (N) elements, and adding amino and carboxyl group modifications on the surface of carbon nanoparticles, the antioxidant, antibacterial and fluorescence properties of carbon nanoparticles were improved. The antioxidant activity of carbon quantum dots was verified by DPPH method, and the antibacterial mechanism of carbon quantum dots was further investigated by surface charge measurement and in vivo study of mouse infected wound model. The results demonstrate that the carbon quantum dots exhibit excellent antibacterial performance, providing a new approach for the development of wound care materials capable of real-time pH monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Wound care
Real-time monitoring
pH-responsive

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The response of NSCQDs to pH values
In vitro antibacterial activity of the NSCQDs

ZK[2024]021, QKH-ZK[2024]440, QKH-ZK[2021]073), Industry and Education Combination Innovation Platform of Intelligent Manufacturing and Graduate Joint Training Base at Guizhou University (Grant No: 2020-520000-83-01-324061), and High-level Creative Talent Training Program in Guizhou Province of China (Grant No. [2015]4015).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Preparation Method of NSCQDs, (b) and (c) TEM images of NSCQDs, (d) FT-IR spectra, (e) X-ray diffraction results, (f) FL emission (blue line) spectra and Ultraviolet–visible (red line) absorbance. Scale bars: 100 nm and 5 nm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Emission fluorescence photographs of NSCQDs solutions at different pH values under dark conditions, from left to right: pH = 5.0, 6.0, 7.0, 8.0, 9.0; (b) Fluorescence emission spectra of NSCQDs in different pH buffer solutions; (c) The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of NSCQDs and pH values (6.0–9.5); (d) The effect of KCl ion strength on the fluorescence intensity of NSCQDs; (e) Antioxidant analysis of NSCQDs using DPPH; (f) Images of dual-fluorescence monitoring hydrogels, from left to right: pH = 3.0, 5.6, 7.4; (g) NSCQDs photos and RGB images at 365 nm radiation for different values of pH; (h) Fitting curves of pH and R, G and B of NSCQDs under UV light.

(a-i) Live/dead staining fluorescent images of fibroblast cells. (a-c) Fluorescent images of fibroblast cells on the groups of PBS after 1 day, 2 days and 3 days of culture repectively. (d-f) Fluorescent images of fibroblast cells on the groups of NSCQD (L) after 1 day, 2 days and 3 days of culture repectively. (g-i) Fluorescent images of fibroblast cells on the groups of NSCQD (M) after 1 day, 2 days and 3 days of culture repectively. The scale bar = 200 μm; (j) CCK-8 assay results.

(a) The results of the zeta potential experiment; (b) Petri dish photo of NSCQDs antibacterial experiment in vitro; (c) and (d) statistical data for S. aureus and E. coli, respectively (****p < 0.0001).

head.ce:title: Synthesis and properties of carbon quantum dots: Antioxidant, antibacterial and pH response monitoring applications

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The pH value of a wound during the healing process significantly impacts the speed and quality of healing. Real-time monitoring of the wound's healing status is crucial for doctors to assess infection and healing conditions and provide appropriate treatment plans. The carbon nanoparticles prepared in this study exhibit good fluorescence properties and pH-responsive characteristics, showing a good linear correlation in the pH range of 5.0 to 9.5. They also possess strong antioxidant and antibacterial properties, making them a promising nanomaterial for real-time pH monitoring and the promotion of wound healing.

By increasing the contents of sulfur (S) and nitrogen (N) elements, and adding amino and carboxyl group modifications on the surface of carbon nanoparticles, the antioxidant, antibacterial and fluorescence properties of carbon nanoparticles were improved. The antioxidant activity of carbon quantum dots was verified by DPPH method, and the antibacterial mechanism of carbon quantum dots was further investigated by surface charge measurement and in vivo study of mouse infected wound model. The results demonstrate that the carbon quantum dots exhibit excellent antibacterial performance, providing a new approach for the development of wound care materials capable of real-time pH monitoring.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a novel type of nanomaterial with a typical size in the range of 1–10 nm. Notably, CQDs possess desirable advantages such as chemical stability, low toxicity, biocompatibility, environmental friendliness, simple synthetic routes, and low cost. Due to these properties, CQDs have received increasing attention for their abundant applications in bioimaging, biosensing, chemical sensing, nanomedicine, photocatalysis, and electrocatalysis [1–5].

Significantly, as a novel fluorescent nanomaterial benefiting from its photoluminescent character, CQDs show potential applications as optical probes [6]. Additionally, CQDs possess properties such as antioxidant, antibacterial, and anti-inflammatory effects, making them useful for the treatment of various diseases [7–9]. Globally, wound healing presents an increasingly serious healthcare challenge. Not only is treating wounds difficult, but chronic wounds that remain in a pathological inflammatory state due to improper healing can further delay recovery. Rather than attempting to cure chronic wounds, a more rational approach is to prevent their formation, which can be achieved by shortening the wound healing time. The success of carbon quantum dots in wound treatment is mainly attributed to their multiple properties, including antibacterial, anti-inflammatory, and antioxidant characteristics.

Meaningfully, CQDs can be functionalized with various surface groups, with oxygen-containing functional groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups commonly present on their surface. This imparts water solubility or dispersibility to carbon quantum dots, allowing them to form stable colloids in water or organic polar solvents. The surface groups also determine the fluorescence properties of the carbon quantum dots. Research has shown that the special edge states at the carbon backbone and CO groups (carbonyl and carboxyl groups) are responsible for the common green fluorescence. The –CO–OH and –CO–N(CH3)2 groups also contribute to green fluorescence (500–520 nm), while the –OH group promotes the emission of blue fluorescence (around 420 nm) [10]. Additionally, surface functional groups can influence the antioxidant properties of carbon quantum dots. Experiments using DPPH· have confirmed that both –COOH and –NH2 groups enhance the antioxidant activity of carbon quantum dots through direct or indirect hydrogen atom transfer reactions with DPPH.

Currently, the antioxidant activity of carbon quantum dots was investigated using the DPPH· reduction method and UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy. DPPH· is one of the best materials for evaluating antioxidant activity due to its excellent stability, high reactivity, and ease of handling. The free radical in DPPH· is stable due to the resonance delocalization of unpaired electrons, with an absorption peak at 517 nm in methanol solution [11]. When hydrogen atoms (or radicals) from antioxidants are accepted, the conjugated π system contracts, leading to a decrease in the intensity of the absorption peak at 517 nm and a color change from deep purple to light yellow [12]. Therefore, the DPPH· method can accurately reflect the antioxidant activity of carbon quantum dots.

Here, we report a novel one-step hydrothermal strategy to acquire a carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with good biocompatibility, having effective antioxidation activity and remarkably fluorescence with pH-responsiveness. The carbon quantum dots were prepared the hydrothermal method within the top-down approach. The top-down method is known for its high quantum yield, mild conditions, and ease of purification and separation. Among these, the hydrothermal method is a very popular and convenient preparation technique. It provides a high-temperature and high-pressure environment, allowing small molecular carbon to aggregate or polymerize into larger carbon quantum dots. Common carbon sources include citric acid, vitamins, proteins, and glucose. This method introduces minimal impurities, and the resulting carbon quantum dots can be used directly.

This study prepared a type of fluorescent carbon quantum dots (NSCQDs) with antioxidant activity that can monitor changes in wound pH using glucose as the carbon source, ethylenediamine as the nitrogen source, and ammonium persulfate as both the sulfur and nitrogen source via the hydrothermal method. Numerous studies have shown that the speed and quality of wound healing are directly related to the pH of the wound environment [13,14]. Monitoring wound pH can help assess and understand the wound status, aiding in the development of better treatment strategies. Establishing a clear link between pH levels and wound healing is particularly significant for developing effective diagnostic techniques to predict and guide wound treatment. Currently, there are various methods for monitoring wound pH, including electrochemical sensors and solid dye dressings [15–17]. However, existing methods have limitations, including instability and high toxicity due to contamination, which restrict their clinical use. pH monitoring based on carbon quantum dots offers advantages such as simplicity of operation, convenience in detection, rapid response, and non-cytotoxicity, providing a new approach for wound pH monitoring in clinical practice. NSCQDs can also be combined with hydrogels, enhancing the hydrogel's ability to promote tissue regeneration and exhibit antibacterial properties in wound care. This study has developed a versatile carbon quantum dot with broad application potential in pH monitoring, antioxidant activity, antibacterial properties, and improving the wound care capabilities of hydrogels.

Carbon dots (CDs) were obtained by the hydrothermal method. First, 0.5 g ammonium persulfate, 0.5 g glucose, and 1.0 mL ethylenediamine were mixed under stirring. When the mixture forms a yellow viscous substance, 10 mL of deionized water was added to obtain a clear yellow solution. The mixture was then placed in a 30 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 2 h, after which it was cooled to room temperature in air. Next, the CDs were collected and purified by dialysis with pure water for 48 h (molecular weight cut-off: 1000) using a dialysis bag. After two rounds of centrifugation (10 min, 5000 rpm, with the addition of alcohol), the purified CDs were collected for further use and stored at 4 °C.

Appropriate amounts of NSCQDs were dissolved in deionized water to prepare low-concentration (NSCQDs (L) = 0.15 g/L), medium-concentration (NSCQDs (M) = 0.3 g/L), and high-concentration (NSCQDs (H) = 1.5 g/L) solutions for further characterization and experiments.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to observe the morphology and lattice structure of NSCQDs. Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS10) was employed to analyze the functional groups present in NSCQDs, while X-ray diffraction (XRD) was used to assess their crystallinity and doping status. UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy experiments were conducted to characterize the optical properties of NSCQDs. The DPPH· assay was utilized to measure the antioxidant activity of NSCQDs, and in vitro antibacterial experiments were performed to verify their antibacterial efficacy. Additionally, cytocompatibility assays and CCK-8 assays were conducted to evaluate the cytotoxicity of NSCQDs, demonstrating their potential for application in wound monitoring.

The antioxidant activity of NSCQDs was determined using the DPPH· method [18]. A 0.04 mg/mL DPPH· solution was prepared by dissolving DPPH· in ethanol. Different concentrations of NSCQDs solutions were also prepared using ethanol as the solvent. A mixture of 2 mL of the DPPH· solution and 2 mL of the NSCQDs solution was incubated in the dark for 1.5 h. Control groups, containing only DPPH· or only NSCQDs, were also prepared. After the reaction, the absorbance of the solution at 517 nm was measured using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer. After excluding the absorbance effect of the carbon quantum dots themselves, the antioxidant activity was calculated as follows:
Antioxidation activity =
A 0
−
A c
A 0
× 100 %
where A₀ is the absorbance of the DPPH· solution without NSCQDs, and AC is the absorbance of the solution containing both DPPH· and NSCQDs at 517 nm.

3 T3-L1 fibroblasts (2 × 104 cells/well) were seeded into a 24-well plate and cultured in DMEM medium supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS). For the control group, PBS solution was added, whereas for the experimental groups, low-concentration (NSCQDs (L) = 75 mg/L) and medium-concentration (NSCQDs (M) = 0.3 g/L) NSCQDs solutions were used. The cells were cultured for 24, 48, and 72 h, and then observed using a fluorescence microscope (Leica DMi8; Germany).

Additionally, 3 T3-L1 fibroblasts (4 × 103 cells/well) were seeded into a 96-well plate and co-cultured with DMEM medium containing 10 % FBS and NSCQDs solution for 24 h. After removing the culture medium, CCK-8 reagent was added, and the cells were incubated for an additional 4 h. The absorbance was measured at 450 nm using a microplate reader (Spectra MAX i3x; Molecular Devices, Sunnyvale, CA, USA).

To evaluate the antibacterial performance of NSCQDs, S. aureus and E. coli were used as bacterial models in a plate-based assay [19]. A bacterial suspension (1.0 × 106 CFU/mL) and NSCQDs (0.3 g/L) samples were added to a 96-well plate and incubated at 37 °C for 1 h (The volume ratio of bacterial suspension to NSCQDs sample was 1:100). After incubation, the solution from each well was diluted 1000-fold and evenly spread onto LB agar plates. The plates were then incubated at 37 °C: S. aureus for 24 h and E. coli for 48 h. Following incubation, the number of bacterial colonies on the LB agar plates was counted. The bacterial survival ratio was calculated using the following formula:
Survival ratio
%
=
CFU S
× 100 %
where CFU represents the number of colony-forming units, and S is the area of the agar plate.

In this experiment, fluorescent carbon quantum dots (NSCQDs) were synthesized using the hydrothermal method with glucose, ammonium persulfate, and ethylenediamine as precursors (Fig. 1a). As shown in Figs. 1b and c) after washing with ethanol, distinct lattice fringes of the NSCQDs were observed, with a lattice spacing of 0.22 nm. This lattice spacing corresponds to the lattice plane of graphene, this indicates that NSCQDs are formed through sp2 hybridization, and the diameter of the carbon dots is within 5 nm, meeting the criteria for quantum dots.

FT-IR spectroscopy was used to characterize the functional groups of NSCQDs. As shown in Fig. 1d), a broad absorption band between 3100 and 3850 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH bonds. The absorption band at 2800–3000 cm−1 is characteristic of CH bonds. The absorption peak at around 2100 cm−1 is due to the stretching vibrations of CN bonds. Another notable FT-IR band appears at 1460 cm−1, corresponding to the CO bond. Additionally, the stretching vibration of SO is located at around 920 cm−1. These bonds indicate that the carbon quantum dots have successfully doped with N and S elements, and also provide molecular evidence that NSCQDs can produce blue-green fluorescence [10]. Fig. 1e) displays the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of NSCQDs, with a peak at 22.8°, representing the [002] plane of carbon, confirming the graphite structure of NSCQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The response of NSCQDs to pH values
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the optical properties of the prepared NSCQDs, UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy experiments were conducted. As shown in Fig. 1f)，the UV–Vis absorption spectrum features an absorption peak centered at 350 nm, which is attributed to the n-π* transition of the CO bond. The fluorescence emission spectrum exhibits strong luminescence in the range of 423–503 nm, corresponding well with the blue-green fluorescence observed in the actual photographs.

The fluorescence intensity of NSCQDs is significantly affected by pH. We took photographs of NSCQDs in solutions with different pH values and measured their fluorescence emission spectra. As shown in Figs. 2a-2c, a noticeable decrease in the fluorescence intensity of NSCQDs was observed as the pH value increased from 5.0 to 9.5. NSCQDs exhibited a good linear relationship in the pH range of 6 to 9.5 with the eq. Y = −4.2115× + 57.673, with a linear correlation coefficient of 0.9845 (Fig. 2c).

As shown in Fig. 2f and Fig. 2g, the color of NSCQDs changes from light blue to dark blue with increasing pH. By this change of color, the healing of the wound can be visually observed. The results indicate that acute wounds are mildly acidic, and when the hydrogel is in an acidic or weakly acidic environment, it emits a noticeable green fluorescence. As the pH value increases, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreases. When the pH is neutral or weakly alkaline, the fluorescence intensity significantly diminishes. This demonstrates the potential application of carbon quantum dots in real-time wound monitoring.

It is reported that the three primary colors of red, green, and blue (RGB) can be combined to create almost any color that can be seen by the naked eye. Current systems for wound pH monitoring include fiberoptic signal measurements and other acid-base indicators. However, fiber signals were susceptible to contamination, and acid-base indicators have certain cytotoxic properties that may yield inaccurate wound pH signals. In this study, we use an image processor to convert, the fluorescence colors of NSCQDs into RGB signals via a corresponding linear equation, which enables the intelligent acquisition of accurate pH for wounds. As shown in Fig. 2h, under the excitation light of 365 nm, when pH increases, the values of R, G and B gradually decrease, and the signal was quantified by calculating the arithmetic average of R, G and B. The specific calculation formula was as follows:

In biological systems, free radicals generated by oxygen metabolism (such as reactive oxygen species (ROS) and singlet oxygen (1O₂)) possess highly reactive oxidative properties due to their unpaired valence electrons. Damage caused by free radicals, including lipid peroxidation, DNA damage, and protein oxidation, can accelerate the aging process. The excessive accumulation of ROS is considered a major cause of inflammation during wound healing [20]. NSCQDs, with their characteristics as electron donors or acceptors, show promise for use as antioxidants [21]. Infected wounds can produce free radicals over an extended period, leading to oxidative stress, which is detrimental to tissue remodeling at the wound site. Studies have shown that hydrogels with free radical scavenging functions (antioxidant properties) can promote wound healing [22]. The carboxyl groups on the surface of NSCQDs can act as proton donors, converting DPPH· into a stable DPPH-H complex [21]. The DPPH· assay was used to determine the free radical scavenging ability of NSCQDs in vitro. The results indicate that as the concentration of NSCQDs increases, their antioxidant activity improves, with a solution of NSCQDs (0.3 g/L) achieving over 70 % antioxidant activity (Fig. 2e).

As a nanomaterial for monitoring wounds, the cytocompatibility of NSCQDs is a necessary prerequisite for their application in wound healing. To investigate the cytocompatibility of NSCQDs, we co-cultured NSCQDs (M), NSCQDs (L), and PBS solution with fibroblasts. Cell viability was assessed on days 1, 2, and 3 to evaluate cytocompatibility. Fluorescence images (Fig. 3 a) show that after three days of culture, a large number of viable cells were present on the surfaces of all three culture media. The number of viable cells in the NSCQDs (M) and NSCQDs (L) groups increased with prolonged culture time (Fig. 3a), with a notable proliferation of cells observed in the NSCQDs (M) group。The CCK-8 assay showed (Fig. 3b) that the cell viability in the NSCQDs (M) and NSCQDs (L) groups was similar to that of the PBS control group, while the NSCQDs (H) group exhibited a lower OD value, indicating that a high concentration of NSCQDs can affect cell proliferation and exhibit cytotoxicity. The fluorescence images and CCK-8 results indicate (Fig. 3) that the NSCQDs concentration in the NSCQDs (M) group has the least cytotoxicity and promotes cell proliferation most effectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro antibacterial activity of the NSCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: We conducted an in-depth study of the sterilization mechanism of NSCQDs. As shown in Fig. 4a), NSCQDs possess a positive charge of 16–20 mV on their surface. This allows NSCQDs to interact with the negatively charged cell membranes of both Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria through electrostatic interactions, disrupting the permeability barrier of the bacterial cell membrane and causing leakage of cellular components, thereby enhancing their antibacterial activity. In vitro experiments (Fig. 4 b) demonstrated that all carbon quantum dot groups exhibited antibacterial effects, with the NSCQDs (M) group showing the best antibacterial efficacy, resulting in almost no colony formation. These findings indicate that the positively charged surface of NSCQDs exerts a strong destructive effect on bacterial cell membranes.

In summary, fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized in a single step using a simple hydrothermal method, employing glucose, ammonium persulfate, and ethylenediamine as raw materials. These CQDs exhibited excellent antioxidant and antibacterial properties, with a good linear relationship observed between pH values of 5.0 and 9.5. The DPPH· assay demonstrated that the CQDs possess the ability to scavenge free radicals, which could potentially reduce wound inflammation. With a surface charge of 16–25 mV, the CQDs can effectively disrupt the permeability barrier of bacterial cell membranes, thereby enhancing their antibacterial effects. This study provides valuable insights into the development of wound care materials with real-time pH monitoring capabilities. The multifunctional properties of the CQDs, including their antioxidant, antibacterial, and pH-responsive characteristics, make them promising candidates for advanced wound care applications.

Jingyi Peng: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Chengjun Hu: Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Xiaohan Zhou: Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Dingyu Wu: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Xun Sun: Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Anqi Tang: Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Xing-Yu Long: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Xiaoqiang Li: Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis. Jie Peng: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Junlong Tian: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by Natural Science Foundation of Hunan in China (no. 2023JJ30588), Guizhou Provincial Basic Research Program (Natural Science) (no. ZK[2024]021, QKH-ZK[2024]440, QKH-ZK[2021]073), Industry and Education Combination Innovation Platform of Intelligent Manufacturing and Graduate Joint Training Base at Guizhou University (Grant No: 2020-520000-83-01-324061), and High-level Creative Talent Training Program in Guizhou Province of China (Grant No. [2015]4015).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An active structure preservation method for developing functional graphitic carbon dots as an effective antibacterial agent and a sensitive pH and Al(III) nanosensor

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Hou, P.; Yang, T.; Liu, H.; Li, Y. F.; Huang, C. Z., An active structure preservation method for developing functional graphitic carbon dots as an effective antibacterial agent and a sensitive pH and Al(III) nanosensor. Nanoscale 2017, 9 (44), 17334-17341.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots with pH-responsive fluorescence: A review on synthesis and cell biological applications

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Smart, Tunable CQDs with Antioxidant Properties for Biomedical Applications-Ecofriendly Synthesis and Characterization

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Janus, L.; Radwan-Praglowska, J.; Piatkowski, M.; Bogdal, D., Smart, Tunable CQDs with Antioxidant Properties for Biomedical Applications-Ecofriendly Synthesis and Characterization. Molecules 2020, 25 (3).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots and their biomedical and therapeutic applications: A review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Molaei, M. J., Carbon quantum dots and their biomedical and therapeutic applications: a review. RSC Adv 2019, 9 (12), 6460-6481.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Insight into the effect of functional groups on visible-fluorescence emissions of graphene quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Du, J.; Wang, H.; Wang, L.; Zhu, S.; Song, Y.; Yang, B.; Sun, H., Insight into the effect of functional groups on visible-fluorescence emissions of graphene quantum dots. Journal of Materials Chemistry C 2016, 4 (11), 2235-2242.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: DPPH Assay Adapted to the FIA System for the Determination of the Antioxidant Capacity of Wines: Optimization of the Conditions Using the Response Surface Methodology

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Bukman, L.; Martins, A. C.; Barizao, E. O.; Visentainer, J. V.; Almeida, V. d. C., DPPH Assay Adapted to the FIA System for the Determination of the Antioxidant Capacity of Wines: Optimization of the Conditions Using the Response Surface Methodology.
